title: AI Has a Control Panel
summary: Why prompt engineering alone is not enough and how parameter strategy shapes AI outputs.
content: If you are designing AI-powered tools, automating workflows, building CV/ATS systems, or embedding LLMs into analytics pipelines, prompt engineering alone is insufficient.
You need parameter strategy. Every response is shaped by configured settings that govern behavior.
Each parameter controls a specific dimension:
- Temperature (0.0 - 1.5): controls creativity versus precision. Low is analytical, high is expressive.
- Max Tokens (about 50 - 200+): controls response length from short summaries to detailed breakdowns.
- Top-p (0.5 - 1.0): controls diversity of word choice. Lower is safer, higher explores broader options.
- Frequency Penalty (0.0 - 2.0): reduces repetition and prevents looping outputs.
- Presence Penalty (0.0 - 2.0): encourages new ideas and pushes beyond one line of thought.
- System Prompt (instruction layer): defines tone, structure, and behavior. The invisible hand shaping every output.
Same model. Different configuration. Completely different outcome.

title: You Don't Have a Query Problem. You Have an Indexing Problem.
summary: Why indexing changes performance more than rewriting queries and how to think about access paths.
content: Most people try to fix slow SQL by rewriting queries. Meanwhile the database is scanning millions of rows just to return a few. That is not optimization.
What indexing actually does: an index is a shortcut into your data. Without it, your database reads everything. With it, your database goes straight to what matters.
What happens when you do not index:
- Full table scans on every query.
- Increased CPU and memory usage.
- Performance that collapses as data grows.
Indexed vs non-indexed (real difference):
No index: scan 1M rows, find 10, wait.
With index: jump to 10 rows, done.
Same query. Different universe.
Some index types to know:
- Clustered index: defines how data is stored (one per table).
- Non-clustered index: separate lookup structure (multiple allowed).
- Composite index: multiple columns (for example user_id + date).
When indexing makes the biggest difference:
- Columns used in WHERE.
- Columns used in JOINs.
- Columns used in ORDER BY.
- Large tables with frequent reads.
If your SQL is slow, do not just optimize queries. Optimize how your data is accessed.

title: Building Health Analytics That Survive Real-World Data Gaps
summary: Practical strategies to design health analytics that remain reliable when data is incomplete or delayed.
content: Health analytics rarely starts with perfect data. Systems are fragmented, reporting is delayed, and facility coverage varies.
This note explains how to design resilient indicators, add quality flags, and protect decision making when gaps exist.
A strong health analytics workflow prioritizes transparency, provenance, and clear decision boundaries.

title: Program Intelligence That Drives Decisions
summary: How to build programme intelligence systems that link data, learning, and operational decisions.
content: Program intelligence turns reporting into decision-ready insight by aligning indicators to stakeholder needs.
This note covers KPI hierarchy, decision cadence, and learning loops that keep programme delivery accountable.
Use the included framework to move from monitoring to operational intelligence.

title: CRISP-DM in Public Programme Delivery Pipelines
summary: How I adapt CRISP-DM phases to match institutional timelines and stakeholder decision windows.
content: This note breaks down how CRISP-DM can be tuned for real-world programme delivery, especially when multiple stakeholders need answers at different times.
I highlight where discovery, data preparation, and evaluation should be compressed or extended to match operational decision windows.
The goal is to keep governance intact while still moving quickly enough to inform action.

title: Choosing Between Descriptive and Predictive Workflows
summary: Decision criteria for selecting fit-for-purpose analysis depth under real operational constraints.
content: Some problems require only clear descriptive insight, while others need full predictive modelling.
This note explains how to decide between the two based on uncertainty, time pressure, and available data quality.
I share a short decision framework used in delivery work to avoid over-engineering.

title: Data Quality Checks Before Dashboard Delivery
summary: A practical checklist for auditability, validation, and source consistency before publishing dashboards.
content: Before any dashboard goes live, I run a short quality checklist that protects stakeholder trust.
This includes source validation, reconciliation against official figures, and checks for missing or biased fields.
The checklist is lightweight but strong enough for audit-ready delivery.

title: Prompt Governance for AI-Assisted Analytics Teams
summary: Prompt standards and review controls that maintain quality when teams adopt generative AI tools.
content: As teams scale AI-assisted analytics, prompt quality becomes a governance issue.
This note outlines prompt standards, review checkpoints, and lightweight QA protocols.
The approach keeps creativity while protecting data integrity and decision quality.
